summary: Branch by Abstraction is a technique for making large-scale changes to software systems incrementally while maintaining continuous delivery capability. The approach involves creating an abstraction layer between client code and a component you want to replace, gradually migrating all clients to use this abstraction, then building a new implementation behind it. This allows the old and new implementations to coexist during migration, with the system remaining deployable throughout. The technique is particularly valuable as an alternative to long-lived version control branches when replacing frameworks or major dependencies.
key_points: Create an abstraction layer that captures interactions between client code and the component being replaced
Gradually migrate all client code to use the abstraction, improving test coverage along the way
Build the new implementation behind the same abstraction, allowing both old and new to coexist
Use feature flags to compare behavior between implementations in test environments
The system remains buildable and deployable throughout the entire migration process
money_quotes: Use an abstraction layer to allow multiple implementations to co-exist in the software system.
Ensure that the system builds and runs correctly at all times, so you can continue to use Continuous Delivery while you are doing the replacement.
Look for as many ways as possible to make changes gradually.
Use the notion of one abstraction and multiple implementations to perform the migration from one implementation to the other.
While we are building the new feature we can use FeatureFlags to run the new supplier in test environments and compare its behavior to the flawed supplier.
key_insight: Large-scale system changes can be made safely and incrementally by introducing an abstraction layer that allows old and new implementations to coexist, enabling continuous delivery throughout the migration.
